article_title: Your NVMe SSD might be running at half speed because of this BIOS setting
core_thesis: Many high-performance NVMe SSDs, especially PCIe Gen 4 models, may run at significantly reduced speeds (often half or less) due to a default BIOS setting that forces the M.2 slot to operate in PCIe Gen 3 mode. This issue is easy to overlook because the drive works normally, and the fix is a simple BIOS change that can double sequential read/write performance.
detailed_summary: The article describes a real-world scenario where a WD Black SN850X Gen 4 NVMe SSD (rated for 7,300 MB/s read, 6,600 MB/s write) was only achieving ~3,651 MB/s read and ~2,839 MB/s write in CrystalDiskMark benchmarks. The root cause was a BIOS setting that forced the M.2 slot to PCIe Gen 3 mode instead of Gen 4 or Auto. The author explains the difference between PCIe generations: Gen 3 x4 caps at ~3,500 MB/s, while Gen 4 x4 doubles to ~7,000 MB/s. The fix involves entering BIOS, navigating to M.2/PCIe configuration (e.g., Advanced > PCIe/M.2 Configuration on MSI boards), and changing the slot's PCIe generation from Gen 3 to Gen 4 or Auto. After the change, the drive reached expected speeds. The article also notes that this setting can affect multiple M.2 slots and that users should check their motherboard manual or search online for exact menu locations. A commenter adds that only the M.2 slot closest to the CPU typically gets full PCIe bandwidth; other slots may be limited by the chipset or shared with the GPU.

entities.name: WD Black SN850X
entities.role: NVMe SSD used as example; rated for 7,300 MB/s read and 6,600 MB/s write

entities.name: CrystalDiskMark
entities.role: Benchmarking tool used to measure SSD sequential read/write speeds

entities.name: MSI Z890 Gaming Plus Wi-Fi
entities.role: Motherboard used in the example; BIOS settings located under Advanced > PCIe/M.2 Configuration

entities.name: Robin John
entities.role: Author of the article, experienced tech journalist

key_data_points: A PCIe Gen 4 x4 NVMe SSD has a theoretical sequential read speed of ~7,000 MB/s, while Gen 3 x4 caps at ~3,500 MB/s. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding hardware bottlenecks is crucial for optimizing data transfer in offensive operations, e.g., exfiltration or loading large payloads.)

The author's WD Black SN850X achieved only 3,651 MB/s read and 2,839 MB/s write when the BIOS was set to Gen 3, roughly half its rated speed. (🟢 Relevant to homelab/DevOps: misconfigured hardware can severely impact performance in data-intensive tasks like log analysis or VM storage.)

The fix is a simple BIOS change: locate the M.2 PCIe mode setting (e.g., M2_1 Gen Mode, PCIe Speed) and switch from Gen 3 to Gen 4 or Auto. (🟢 Relevant to programming/DevOps: knowing BIOS tweaks helps in building high-performance development environments.)

A commenter notes that only the M.2 slot closest to the CPU gets full PCIe bandwidth; other slots may be limited by the chipset or shared with the GPU. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: when building a server or workstation, slot selection matters for maximum storage performance.)
methodology_steps: 1. Download and install CrystalDiskMark (free) to benchmark your NVMe SSD. Run a sequential read/write test and note the SEQ1M Q8T1 and SEQ1M Q1T1 results.
2. Compare your results to the drive's rated speed (check manufacturer's product page). If a Gen 4 drive shows ~3,500 MB/s or less, the BIOS is likely forcing Gen 3.
3. Restart your PC and enter BIOS (usually Delete, F2, or F12 during startup).

4. Navigate to advanced settings and look for M.2 or PCIe configuration options. On MSI boards, this is under Advanced > PCIe/M.2 Configuration. For other brands (ASUS, Gigabyte, ASRock), search for your motherboard model + 'PCIe M.2 BIOS setting'.
5. Find the PCIe generation setting for the slot your SSD is installed in (e.g., M2_1, M2_2). If unsure, check the Easy Mode section of BIOS to identify the slot.
6. Change the setting from Gen 3 to Gen 4, or set it to Auto if available.
7. Save and exit BIOS. The PC will restart.
8. Re-run CrystalDiskMark to confirm speeds have doubled. If not, verify the slot is the primary one (closest to CPU) and not shared with other devices.
conclusions: A default BIOS setting forcing PCIe Gen 3 mode is a common cause of half-speed NVMe SSDs, and the fix is straightforward: change the M.2 slot's PCIe generation to Gen 4 or Auto. This can double sequential read/write performance, as demonstrated by the author's SN850X jumping from ~3,500 MB/s to ~7,000 MB/s.

Users should not assume a slow SSD is defective; checking BIOS settings is a quick diagnostic step before opening a support ticket. This is especially relevant for homelab and DevOps setups where storage performance directly impacts workflow efficiency.

For maximum performance, install the NVMe SSD in the M.2 slot closest to the CPU, as other slots may be limited by chipset bandwidth or shared with the GPU. This is a key consideration when building or upgrading a system for data-intensive tasks.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, as it provides a concrete, actionable fix for a common hardware bottleneck that can halve SSD performance. As a programmer and Red Teamer, you rely on fast storage for compiling code, running VMs, and handling large datasets; ensuring your NVMe runs at full Gen 4 speeds can significantly reduce wait times. The step-by-step BIOS guide is practical for any custom build, and the comment about slot selection is a pro-tip for optimizing your homelab server. Additionally, understanding hardware limitations like PCIe generation is useful for Red Teaming scenarios where data exfiltration speed matters.
extra_advice: Consider adding a BIOS configuration checklist to your homelab provisioning scripts or documentation. For Red Teaming, test data transfer rates under different PCIe modes to quantify the impact on exfiltration times. Also, if you use multiple NVMe drives, verify each slot's PCIe generation and lane allocation to avoid unexpected bottlenecks in parallel workloads.
